metadata.summary: SPIN Selling, Challenger Sale, elevator pitch templates, and LAER objection handling for persuasively pitching ideas to diverse audiences.

content.title: Selling Ideas
content.summary: A persuasion framework for pitching technical or business ideas to different audiences. Combines SPIN Selling (uncover and amplify pain before presenting a solution), the Challenger Sale (teach a new insight, tailor, take control), the elevator pitch template, and the LAER objection-handling model. The core rule: never pitch until the audience feels the problem.

why.p: Great ideas fail because of bad pitches. Technical people default to feature explanations instead of problem-then-benefit narratives. SPIN research (Rackham, 35,000 sales calls) shows that implication questions — not product descriptions — are what move decision-makers. Matching pitch structure to audience role (executive vs technical vs finance) doubles comprehension and acceptance rates.

list.item: Proposing a technical change, architecture decision, or new tooling to stakeholders
Pitching a product idea to investors, clients, or executives
Preparing for a presentation where you need a decision, not just understanding
Writing a one-pager or executive summary that must persuade
When the audience has already decided — objection handling mode, not pitching mode
Exploratory conversations where no decision is needed yet — use active-listening instead
When you lack numbers or evidence — pitching without proof reads as speculation
Pure technical briefings where the audience wants depth, not persuasion structure

rule.statement: Never pitch the solution until Implication questions have made the problem feel urgent to the audience.
rule.rationale: Rackham's research on 35,000 sales calls showed that Implication questions — not product descriptions — are the primary driver of decision-maker movement. Features presented before pain is amplified produce "that's interesting" responses, not decisions.

list.item: Situation (S): understand current context — "How do you currently handle X?"
Problem (P): uncover pain — "What challenges do you face with X?"
Implication (I): amplify consequences — "What happens if this isn't solved?"
Need-Payoff (N): lead to solution — "How would it help if you could...?"

example.title: Selling a CI/CD pipeline to an engineering manager
example.code:
S: "How do you currently deploy to production?" "Manual process, takes about 2 hours"
P: "What challenges does that create?" "Engineers hate it, we deploy less often"
I: "What happens when you deploy less often?" "Bigger releases, more bugs, more rollbacks" "How much does a rollback cost?" "Usually half a day of engineering time"
N: "If deployments took 10 minutes and were automatic, how would that change things?" "We could deploy daily, catch issues early..." → Now present the CI/CD solution

rule.statement: Use the Teach-Tailor-Take-Control sequence when the audience does not yet know they have a problem: share a surprising insight, connect it to their situation, then guide the decision.
rule.rationale: Challengers win by reframing the buyer's understanding of their own problem. Mere problem-solvers compete on features; Challengers compete on perspective.

example.title: Teach: the reframe insight
example.code: "Most teams think more testing slows them down. But data shows teams with >80% coverage deploy 3x faster. Here's why: they catch bugs before production, not during 2am incidents."

example.title: Tailor: connect to their context
example.code: Connect the insight to: - Their industry and company size - Their specific challenges mentioned in Situation questions - Their competitor pressures

example.title: Take Control: guide the recommendation
example.code:
"Based on what you've shared, I'd recommend option A. Here's why..."
Don't be afraid to disagree if you have better data — that's what makes it a recommendation, not a proposal.

rule.statement: Use the 7-line elevator pitch template to ensure every pitch covers: target customer, problem, category, key benefit, competitor contrast, and differentiator.
rule.rationale: Omitting any element produces a pitch that either confuses the audience about who this is for (no target), fails to establish urgency (no problem), or cannot be remembered (no differentiator).

rule.statement: Match pitch structure to the audience role: executives need business impact first, technical audiences need architecture before benefits, finance needs cost savings with payback period.
rule.rationale: Each role has a primary decision filter. Leading with the wrong frame (e.g., technical architecture to an executive) triggers disengagement before the key point lands.

list.item: Technical: Problem (1m) → Architecture (2m) → Demo (5m) → Integration (2m) → Questions
Finance: Cost savings → TCO analysis → Payback period → Risk
Users: Time savings → Ease of use → Daily workflow change
Security: Compliance → Data protection → Audit trail

rule.statement: Use LAER (Listen, Acknowledge, Explore, Respond) for every objection — never skip Explore before Responding.
rule.rationale: Responding before exploring treats the stated objection as the real one. The real objection is usually different: "too expensive" often means "I don't see the ROI" not "we have no budget".

example.code: Objection: "Too expensive" L: Let them finish. A: "That's a valid concern." E: "Compared to what? What's the cost of not solving this?" R: "Let's calculate the TCO including the current manual cost..."
Objection: "We tried before" E: "What was different then? What would need to change for it to work now?"

rule.statement: Structure presentations as: Hook → Problem → Solution → Proof → Call to Action. End with a specific ask, never an open-ended close.
rule.rationale: Presentations without a clear ask produce "let me think about it" deferrals. The ask defines what decision the audience is making.

antipattern.description: Leading with the solution before establishing the problem.
antipattern.reason: Audiences who do not yet feel the pain perceive solutions as unnecessary complexity. Pain must be established first.

antipattern.description: Asking "Does that make sense?" after presenting.
antipattern.reason: Invites a yes/no response that shuts down dialogue. Use "What questions do you have?" instead.

antipattern.description: Using weak modal verbs: "I think we should...", "Maybe we could..."
antipattern.reason: Modality signals uncertainty, which undermines the recommendation. Use "I recommend..." and "Here's the plan..."

section.name: agent-integration-guidelines
section.p: An agent receives an idea brief (problem, solution, audience type, known objections, available metrics). It produces: (1) a SPIN question sequence to run before the pitch, (2) an audience-tailored pitch doc (problem → solution → impact → ask), (3) an objection-response map for up to 5 known objections using LAER, (4) an elevator pitch in three lengths. Each output is a separate section, clearly labeled, so the human can selectively use them.

rule.statement: Always lead with the Problem, not the Solution — agents default to solution-first because specs are usually solution-first; explicitly reverse the order in the prompt.
rule.rationale: SPIN question sequences need authentic human delivery; agent-written SPIN scripts read as interrogative rather than curious.

rule.statement: The Challenger Sale "Teach" step requires genuinely novel industry insight — agents will produce plausible-sounding but often generic "insights" that sophisticated buyers will recognize as boilerplate.
rule.rationale: Objection handling scripts generated without real objection data tend to address imagined concerns, not actual ones.

rule.statement: Quantification ("40% lower TCO") requires real data; agents will hallucinate specifics if not given source numbers.
rule.rationale: Agents will invent specific percentages when not given real data — always mark claims as [DATA NEEDED] in the prompt and review before use.

rule.statement: Build an objection library over time: after each pitch, log real objections raised and effective responses. Feed this library into future agent prompts.
rule.rationale: For executive pitches: one metric per slide, no more. An agent will fill space with data; trim ruthlessly.

rule.statement: The Elevator Pitch 10-second version ("We help [WHO] [DO WHAT] [BETTER/FASTER/CHEAPER]") is the hardest to write well and the most important; dedicate a separate prompt pass to it.
rule.rationale: SPIN sequences work best when the Implication questions quantify cost ("How much engineering time does a rollback cost?") — ensure the brief includes actual cost data, even rough estimates.

rule.statement: "Ask" statements generated by agents are often weak ("Let's schedule a follow-up") — specify what type of commitment you want (time / budget / referral) in the prompt.
rule.rationale: Power-phrase substitutions (Weak → Strong) can sound aggressive if applied wholesale; human review needed to calibrate tone per audience.

rule.statement: SPIN question sequences generated without audience context will produce Situation questions that are too broad and Implication questions too generic to create urgency.
rule.rationale: Live negotiation cannot be handled by agents that cannot adapt to in-room dynamics in real time; agents can prepare but not participate live.

template.purpose: Fill-in-the-blank elevator pitch template (7-line and 10-second versions)
One-pager structure: Problem / Solution / Benefits / Cost / Risk / Ask
4-block executive pitch: Problem / Solution / Impact / Ask (30 sec each)
